environment.hardware: No special hardware required for anything checked here. The LaTeX recompile (pdflatex -> bibtex -> pdflatex x2) is a standard single-machine build with no external figure assets (only tikz-cd/tikz diagrams). The two spot-checked Haskell suites are small (N<=8 dense exact diagonalization, i.e. matrices up to 256x256, for spectral-gap-stability; a 2000-point momentum-grid sweep for bordism-realizability's Kitaev chain) and both built and ran in well under a minute on Apple Silicon (aarch64) macOS 25.5.0 (Darwin); no GPU or cluster needed.
environment.software: pdfTeX 3.141592653-2.6-1.40.29 (TeX Live 2026) with BibTeX 0.99e, run against a scratch copy of synthesis.tex + references.bib outside the tracked papers/latex/ directory so no .aux/.log/.bbl artifacts were left in the original tree (confirmed clean afterward). GHC 9.14.1 and cabal-install 3.16.1.0 (Homebrew) to build and run the two spot-checked Haskell packages. Poppler's pdftotext to verify rendered citations and Recollection/Conjecture numbering in the compiled PDF.
dependencies: QuickCheck (bordism-realizability.cabal pins >=2.14,<2.19; spectral-gap-stability.cabal leaves it unconstrained), resolved from the local Hackage/cabal cache without any manual index update
base (GHC boot package; both TFIM.hs and Kitaev.hs are otherwise dependency-free per their own docstrings, confirmed by inspecting their import lists)

Standard TeX Live 2026 packages for synthesis.tex itself: amsmath/amssymb/amsfonts/amsthm, tikz-cd/tikz (+calc), array/booktabs, needspace/placeins, hyperref/cleveref, graphicx, geometry, xcolor, aliascnt -- all resolved with no manual install

concerns: synthesis.tex (Part VI) itself contains zero references to code, Haskell, QuickCheck, src/, or any code/data availability statement -- grep for 'haskell|companion code|accompanying.*suite|computational verif|src/' over the paper returns no hits at all. Every computational claim is inherited implicitly through the Recollections attributed to Parts I-V (in particular Recollection III/IIIc, citing Theorems III-A/B/C, and Recollection VA/VB/VC, citing Theorems V-A/B/C) and is never itself re-cited as 'verified by the accompanying suite' anywhere in this paper. A reader of only this PDF has no in-paper pointer to src/spectral-gap-stability/ or src/bordism-realizability/, and the classification above (unspecified) reflects that gap in this paper specifically, not the wider project, which does ship open MIT-licensed code.

Built and ran both suggested spot-checks directly (GHC 9.14.1 + cabal-install 3.16.1.0). src/spectral-gap-stability/ ('cabal run sgs-demo') builds clean and prints the TFIM gap table with the 1-gap at the discriminant g=1 shrinking as system size grows (0.6946, 0.4821, 0.3691 for N=4, 6, 8) -- the gap-closing behaviour Recollection III's stability/continuity language depends on -- and passes 127/127 deterministic theorem instances plus every QuickCheck property. src/bordism-realizability/ ('cabal run bordism-realizability') builds clean and prints the Kitaev-chain sweep with the winding number and Majorana number flipping together exactly at |mu|=2|t|=2 (trivial <-> topological) and phase-boundary detection matching the analytic 2t to within about 0.1%, passing 21/21 deterministic checks and 10/10 properties. Both results genuinely support the module-paper claims synthesis.tex recollects, but neither the code nor its output is referenced anywhere in synthesis.tex itself.

Table 1 (tab:conjectures) lists 19 module-level conjecture IDs (I-1..I-3, II-1..II-5, III-1..III-3, IV-1..IV-5, V-1..V-3) with abbreviated statements. Checked every ID against the actual conjecture environments in all five module .tex files: all 19 exist with matching topics, and the count (3+5+3+5+3=19) matches the abstract's and Section 6's claim of 'nineteen module conjectures.' The 'Depends on' dependency column was spot-checked on a handful of entries rather than exhaustively verified against each module paper's own stated prerequisites.

The five module papers use three different LaTeX mechanisms for their own conjecture numbering, which Table 1 flattens into one uniform 'X-k' scheme -- consistent in content, but not in how each source paper actually generates the number. positivity-cstar-norms.tex (Part II) auto-numbers correctly via \renewcommand{\theconjecture}{II-\arabic{conjecture}}. spectral-gap-stability.tex (Part III) sidesteps the issue with three unnumbered environments whose names are literally 'Conjecture III-1/2/3'. lattice-eft-equivalence.tex (Part IV) uses one generic unnumbered \newtheorem*{conjecture}{Conjecture} with 'IV-k' only in the manual optional title. But lieb-robinson-locality.tex (Part I) and bordism-realizability.tex (Part V) both declare \newtheorem{conjecture}[theorem]{Conjecture}, sharing the ordinary theorem/proposition counter, so each conjecture's real auto-generated number (e.g. 'Conjecture 3.2') differs from the 'I-1'/'V-1' string that is only manually embedded in the bracketed title -- a within-paper numbering inconsistency in two of the five modules. The 'I-k'/'V-k' identifier is still present and findable as title text, so Table 1's content correspondence is not actually broken by it, but a reader of Part I or Part V's own PDF sees two different numbers attached to the same conjecture.

Only 3 of the 5 module papers cited by synthesis.tex have a compiled .pdf sitting next to their .tex in papers/latex/ (lattice-eft-equivalence.pdf, positivity-cstar-norms.pdf, spectral-gap-stability.pdf); lieb-robinson-locality.tex (Part I) and bordism-realizability.tex (Part V) have no corresponding .pdf there, so their build state could only be checked by source inspection, not by re-examining a pre-existing compiled artifact. This does not affect synthesis.tex's own build, which depends only on references.bib, not on the other five .tex files being present or compiled.
